title: Electricity
subject: science
audioScript: Welcome to the Electricity chapter review. Electric current is the flow of charge through a conductor. Ohm's law states that voltage equals current times resistance: V equals I R. In series circuits, resistances add directly. In parallel circuits, the reciprocal of total resistance equals the sum of reciprocals. Electrical power is P equals V I. Joule heating explains why filaments glow in bulbs. Review the circuit diagram and verify unit consistency in every numerical example.

sections.title: 1. Concept Overview
sections.body: Electric current is the rate of flow of electric charge. Potential difference (voltage) drives charge through a conductor. Resistance opposes current. CBSE Class 10 focuses on Ohm's law, series and parallel combinations, heating effect, and electric power in household circuits.

sections.title: 2. Key Definitions
sections.body:
- **Electric current (I):** Rate of flow of charge; unit ampere (A).
- **Potential difference (V):** Work done per unit charge; unit volt (V).
- **Resistance (R):** Opposition to current; unit ohm (Ω).
- **Resistivity:** Material property; R = ρL/A.

sections.title: 4. Formulas / Theorems
sections.body:
- Ohm's law: **V = IR**
- Power: **P = VI = I²R = V²/R**
- Series: **R = R₁ + R₂ + …**
- Parallel: **1/R = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + …**
- Joule heating: **H = I²Rt**

sections.title: 12. Teacher Explanation Module
sections.body: Think of electric current like water in a pipe. Voltage is the pressure pushing the water. Resistance is a narrow section of the pipe. Ohm's law simply says: more pressure or wider pipe means more flow — unless resistance increases. When students confuse series and parallel, draw both side by side and count how many paths the current can take.
